title: Catechist's Guide — Anointing of the Sick
html:
Catechist's Guide

For preparing and leading the lesson. For exclusive use of the catechist.

Lesson Summary

Estimated duration: 50-60 minutes (with activities, 90 minutes)

Central theme: The Anointing of the Sick is a sacrament of healing and hope, not merely a rite for the dying. Christ the physician touches the sick person, strengthens them with the Holy Spirit, unites their suffering to his redemptive Passion, and prepares them —if necessary— for the Father's house.

Objectives:

  • The student understands that this sacrament is for the sick, not only for the dying (CCC 1511).
  • The student knows the biblical foundation: James 5 and the Gospels.
  • The student understands the effects of the sacrament according to the Catechism (CCC 1520-1523).
  • The student discovers the connection with Viaticum and the final sacraments.
  • The student appreciates the cultural richness: saints, art, and testimonies.

Key verse: Jas 5:14-15

Catechism references: CCC 1499-1532

Suggested Lesson Structure

Section Duration What the catechist does
1. Welcome and icebreaker 5 min Welcome, create a warm atmosphere. Ask: «Have you ever been truly sick? Who took care of you?» Listen without judgment.
2. The story of St. Camillus de Lellis 8 min Tell the story of the gambling soldier who saw Christ in the sick. Use Camillus's phrase: «In the sick I see Christ, and in Christ I see the sick.» Ask: «Have you ever found God in someone who was suffering?»
3. Main content 25 min Present the 5 sections. Read Jas 5:14-15 and Mk 6:12-13 together. Explain: matter (oil), form (prayer), minister (priest/bishop), effects (5 gifts). Clarify the name change (Extreme Unction → Anointing of the Sick). Explain Viaticum and the three final sacraments.
4. Group activity 12 min Choose an activity from the workbook. Children: draw the cross with names of the sick. Youth/adults: prepare a dialogue with a sick person explaining the sacrament without fear.
5. Sharing 7 min Students share what they discovered. Optional: don't force it.
6. Final prayer and closing 3 min Pray together the prayer from the workbook. Recall the verse from James.

Notes for the Catechist

About St. Camillus de Lellis

St. Camillus de Lellis (1550-1614) is one of the most fascinating saints in Church history. Born into a noble family in the Abruzzi region of Italy, his youth was a disaster: mercenary soldier, compulsive gambler, violent. An ulcer on his leg brought him to the Hospital of St. James in Rome, where he hit bottom —and where he found his vocation. He founded the Order of the Ministers of the Sick (the Camillians), who revolutionized hospital care with one principle: treat every sick person as if they were Christ. They invented practices now obvious but then unheard of: ventilating rooms, changing bed linens, separating contagious patients. Camillus is patron of the sick, of hospitals, and of healthcare workers. He died at 64 and his last words were: «I rejoice at having been crucified with the Lord.»

For further study: The biography of St. Camillus by Fr. Mario Vanti offers moving details. His life is also a testimony that no one is too lost for grace.

About the Anointing as a sacrament of healing

It is crucial that students understand the change in perspective brought by the Second Vatican Council (Constitution Sacrosanctum Concilium 73). Before 1963, common practice was to call this sacrament «Extreme Unction» and administer it only in imminent danger of death —often when the sick person was already losing consciousness. The vast majority of the faithful died without having received it, or received it without knowing. The Council recovered the original name and restored its meaning: it is a sacrament for the conscious sick person, to strengthen them in their struggle, not to bid them farewell. This pastoral point is fundamental: if students leave with the idea that «the Anointing is for those who are already about to die,» they will never request it for themselves or their family members.

About suffering as participation in the Cross

This is perhaps the most difficult point to convey. Human suffering, in itself, is an evil. We must not glorify it or seek it. But when suffering comes —and it comes to all of us— the Christian faith offers something radically different from stoicism («grin and bear it») or nihilism («it's meaningless»). It offers the possibility of uniting suffering to Christ's, transforming it into an act of redemptive love. It is not that Christ needs our pain; it is that he loves us so much that he allows us to participate in his saving work. St. Paul's letter to the Colossians (Col 1:24) is fundamental here, as is the encyclical Salvifici doloris of St. John Paul II (1984). Don't force this idea; propose it with tenderness.

Age Adaptation

🌱 Children (7-12): The image of «Jesus the doctor» works very well. Ask: «What does a doctor do when you're sick? He touches you, gives you medicine, tells you you'll get better. Jesus is even better: he touches you with blessed oil, gives you his strength, and never leaves you alone.» The cross activity with names of the sick is ideal for them. It can be done with crayons or markers. They can also dramatize the Good Samaritan parable.

🌿 Youth (13-17): Connect with their experience of fragility: «Have you ever been afraid? Have you ever been alone in a hospital?» The testimony of Blessed Carlo Acutis —canonized in 2025— who offered his illness with joy, is very powerful for them. Also the story of the young Camillus, who felt like a failure before his conversion. Use the «Catequizis» video by Juan Manuel Cotelo on the Anointing (Infinito + 1).

🪴 New adults: Skepticism about this sacrament is very common: «Isn't this only for when you're about to die?» Explain the Vatican II change clearly. The experiential argument: «When you are truly sick, what do you desire most? Someone to be with you. The Anointing is exactly that: Christ himself being with you.»

🌳 Formed adults: Deepen the theology of Christian suffering: St. Thomas's desiderium naturale applied to the final end, the distinction between physical healing and spiritual salvation, the concept of Viaticum as the culmination of Christian initiation. St. John Paul II and Benedict XVI (encyclical Spe salvi) are key references. The ecclesial dimension of suffering (CCC 1522) often sparks good discussion.

About Viaticum

Many Catholics have never heard this word. Explain the etymology: viaticum = provision for the journey. The Eucharist is food for the road, and at the final moment it is food to cross the frontier of death. It is important that students know the three final sacraments have a logical order: Penance (forgiveness), Anointing (strength), Eucharist (nourishment). It is a summary of Christian life.

Supplementary Resources

  • YouCat 241-248: The Youth Catechism has a brief and clear section on the Anointing.
  • Opus Dei, Theme 17: The series «Themes of the Christian Faith» from Opus Dei at opusdei.org includes the topic of the Anointing.
  • Infinito + 1 (Juan Manuel Cotelo): The «Catequizis» series on YouTube. The episode on the Anointing is moving and clear.
  • Ascension Presents (Fr. Mike Schmitz): Very accessible videos on this sacrament.
  • Padre Luis Toro: YouTube catechesis in Spanish with direct language for youth and adults.
  • Salvifici doloris (St. John Paul II, 1984): A brief and profound apostolic letter. Recommended reading for catechists.

Possible Difficult Questions (and How to Answer Them)

«If I get anointed, does that mean I'm going to die?»

No. That is precisely the idea the Church has corrected. The Anointing is not an announcement of death; it is a sacrament to ask for strength and healing. It can be received several times in one's life. Many people receive it before major surgery and then live many more years. The priest is not there to bid you farewell; he is there to bring you Christ's grace that fights alongside you.

«Why doesn't God cure me? I've prayed so much and I'm still sick»

This is a deeply painful question and there is no easy answer. You might say: «I don't know why God allows this particular illness to continue. But I do know that God is not indifferent to your suffering. Look at him on the cross: he didn't stay in heaven giving explanations. He came down, suffered himself, and now accompanies you. The Anointing is not a guarantee of physical cure —though that can happen— but the certainty that Christ is with you in the illness.» Do not try to resolve the mystery of suffering in three minutes. Accompany.

«My relative is very ill, but I don't want to call the priest because they'll be frightened and give up»

This is the most common fear. The pastoral response is clear: if the sick person is conscious, you must explain to them beforehand what the Anointing means —that it is a sacrament of life, of strength, not of death. If the sick person is unconscious, the Church permits anointing someone in danger even if they cannot express themselves. But the ideal is to prepare the sick person in time: «Dad/Mom, I'm going to call the priest to bring you the blessing of the sick. It's so God gives you strength.» The tone changes everything.

«What's the difference between Anointing and just praying for the sick? Aren't they the same?»

They are not the same. Private prayer is powerful and necessary. But the Anointing is a sacrament: an action of Christ himself through his Church, with an efficacy guaranteed by the Lord's promise. When I pray for a sick person, I ask God to help them. When the Church administers the Anointing, Christ himself acts through the priest. It does not depend on the faith of the minister or the sick person —though faith disposes one better. It is Christ who acts. That is why the Anointing is irreplaceable.

Catechist's Prayer

Lord Jesus, divine physician who touched lepers
and gave sight to the blind,
give me a heart like the Good Samaritan's:
that I may know how to stop before the one who suffers,
bandage their wounds with tenderness,
and pour the oil of your consolation.
Do not let my words drive away,
but rather draw them to you.
May the sick I encounter today
see in me —just a little— your face.
Amen.

Print Catechist's Guide
